diagnosis.score_summary: With a 0/100 alignment score, this is a case of brand abandonment, not inconsistency. A fully-formed, complex personality exists on the Amazon PDP, while the brand's own WWW presence is a complete void, ceding 100% of brand definition to a third-party marketplace.

truth_1.headline: A Medical Authority With No Office
truth_1.gap_description: The PDP positions the brand as 'The Medical Authority,' a trusted, professional, and utilitarian choice. The WWW has no positioning at all. The brand claims expertise on a marketplace but doesn't have a home address for customers to verify it.
truth_1.evidence: [POSITIONING] WWW: No Position vs. PDP: 'The Medical Authority' — 'A clean, professional studio shot...The color palette of white, teal, and gold...effectively signals both powerful germ protection and the brand's signature moisturizing care.'
truth_1.why_matters: Authority requires a source of truth. When the only 'official' presence is on a crowded marketplace, the 'Medical Authority' claim feels borrowed and untrustworthy, eroding customer confidence and pricing power.
truth_1.the_hard_choice: You have accidentally built a brand on rented land. The choice is now brutally simple. Option A: Fully commit to 'The Medical Authority' persona and immediately build a WWW experience that serves as its clinical, authoritative home base. Option B: Decide this persona is wrong, pull the plug on the successful Amazon listing, and start the long process of building a new identity from scratch. You can't be a doctor who only works out of a flea market.

truth_2.headline: Solving Problems for Strangers
truth_2.gap_description: On Amazon, the brand is a confident 'Problem Solver,' using rational, benefit-focused hooks to convert customers. On its own website, the brand is silent. It has a powerful sales pitch but no story, no 'about us,' no soul.
truth_2.evidence: [MESSAGING] WWW: No Message vs. PDP: 'The Problem Solver' — 'banish bacteria*, keep skin soft!...eliminates 99% of bacteria in a clinical wash test vs. E.coli'
truth_2.why_matters: A brand defined only by its sales pitch has no loyalty. Customers buy the solution, not the brand. Without a core narrative on WWW, you are a commodity, vulnerable to any competitor who tells a better story.
truth_2.the_hard_choice: Your Amazon page is a great advertisement, but it's not a brand. You must decide if this ad is the foundation of your identity. Option A: Embrace the 'Problem Solver' role and build a WWW narrative around the science and efficacy proven on Amazon. Option B: Argue that the brand is more than just a function, and build a story-led WWW that forces the Amazon messaging into a secondary, supporting role. Your product has a voice, but your company does not.

truth_3.headline: Expert-Backed, But Self-Abandoned
truth_3.gap_description: The PDP's entire argument rests on powerful 'Derm-Validation'—leading with expert recommendations and clinical stats. The WWW presents no claims at all. Your strongest reasons to believe are completely absent from your own home.
truth_3.evidence: [KEY SELLING POINTS] WWW: No KSPs vs. PDP: 'Derm-Validation' — 'DERMATOLOGIST RECOMMENDED...eliminates 99% of bacteria*'
truth_3.why_matters: This makes the brand look illegitimate. A consumer who cross-references the bold claims on Amazon with a non-existent website will assume the product is either a knock-off or the claims are fake. Trust evaporates instantly.
truth_3.the_hard_choice: Your most valuable asset—expert validation—is being squandered. The choice is about credibility. Option A: Build a WWW that is a temple to 'Derm-Validation,' featuring white papers, expert testimonials, and the data to back up the Amazon claims. Option B: Decide a different KSP is more important and begin establishing it on WWW, knowing it will initially conflict with your high-performing Amazon page. You either own your proof, or it owns you.

truth_4.headline: The Accidental Pragmatist
truth_4.gap_description: While there is zero alignment, the PDP has successfully carved out a clear identity for its user: 'The Pragmatist.' This is a potential bright spot because it proves the brand *can* stand for something. The failure is that this identity exists in a vacuum, completely disconnected from a central brand strategy.
truth_4.evidence: [SELF-EXPRESSION] WWW: No Identity vs. PDP: 'The Pragmatist' — 'Who else would you trust, but the No.1 dermatologist-recommended brand...#6 in Bath & Shower Gels'
truth_4.why_matters: An accidental identity is better than no identity, but it's a strategic liability. This 'Pragmatist' persona now sets customer expectations. Any future attempt to build a more emotional or aspirational brand will clash with this established, utilitarian foundation.
truth_4.the_hard_choice: You've unintentionally typecast your customer. Now you must decide if you like the role. Option A: Formally adopt 'The Pragmatist' as your target audience and align all future brand development around this identity of smart, rational utility. Option B: Reject this accidental persona and launch a WWW with a conflicting identity, accepting the short-term confusion to steer the brand toward a more intentional future. An identity you don't choose is a prison.

truth_5.headline: A Hype Beast in a Library
truth_5.gap_description: The brand's greatest potential strength is the voice it found on Amazon: a witty, energetic 'Hype Beast.' This tone is converting customers in a competitive environment. The weakness is that this voice is trapped on the PDP, while the brand's own channel is completely silent.
truth_5.evidence: [TONE OF VOICE] WWW: Silent vs. PDP: 'The Hype Beast' — 'Get out of here! Banish bacteria from your scared skingdom...What a concept!...Win-Win'
truth_5.why_matters: This proves your brand can have a personality that connects and sells. By keeping this voice siloed on Amazon, you are treating your most charismatic salesperson like a temporary contractor instead of the CEO. Brand equity is being built, but not captured.
truth_5.the_hard_choice: Your marketplace team has discovered fire, but the rest of the company is living in the dark. Option A: Recognize the 'Hype Beast' voice as a proven asset, codify it, and make it the official global tone for the brand, starting with a new WWW. Option B: Classify the Amazon voice as a rogue tactic and develop a safer, corporate voice for the official brand, risking the loss of the very personality that's driving success. You either trust the voice that sells, or you silence it.

marker_1.so_what: The brand's purpose as 'The Guardian' is clearly articulated on Amazon but is entirely absent on its own website, creating a foundational identity crisis.

marker_2.so_what: A 'Democratizers' mission exists on the PDP, but with no WWW presence, the brand has no platform to communicate its vision, making it appear purely transactional.

marker_3.so_what: The brand's 'Safety First' values are detailed on Amazon but are nowhere to be found on an official site, undermining the very trust these values are meant to build.

marker_4.so_what: The 'Medical Authority' positioning on Amazon is left unsupported and unsubstantiated by a non-existent WWW, weakening its credibility.

marker_5.so_what: A vibrant 'Trendsetter' personality engages customers on the PDP, while the core brand remains faceless and mute, creating a jarring disconnect.

marker_6.so_what: A rich 'Caregiver/Jester' archetype mix exists on Amazon, while the brand's own channel has no personality at all, forcing the marketplace to define the brand's soul.

marker_7.so_what: A proven, effective 'Hype Beast' tone of voice on Amazon has no corresponding home on WWW, siloing the brand's most engaging communication asset.

marker_8.so_what: A clear 'Pharma-Code' visual system is used on Amazon, but the brand has no visual identity on its home turf, making it look unprofessional and illegitimate.

marker_9.so_what: Powerful 'Problem Solver' messaging drives conversion on Amazon, but the absence of a core brand story on WWW reduces the brand to a simple sales pitch.

marker_10.so_what: A compelling 'Lab Breakthrough' narrative exists on the PDP, but with no official home, the story feels unowned and potentially inauthentic.

marker_11.so_what: The brand's core 'Derm-Validation' proof points are abandoned on a marketplace, missing the opportunity to build deep authority on a brand-owned platform.

marker_12.so_what: A 'Tech Engineer' USP based on a proprietary blend is explained on Amazon but lacks the deeper storytelling and validation a WWW could provide.

marker_13.so_what: The 'Hard Product Sell' focus on Amazon is effective but one-dimensional; the lack of a WWW prevents any brand-building or community-focused content.

marker_14.so_what: The key functional benefit of 'Protection' is clear on the PDP, but the brand fails to own this narrative on a central, authoritative website.

marker_15.so_what: The emotional benefit of 'Relief / Safety' is communicated on Amazon but is not reinforced by a broader brand experience, limiting its impact.

marker_16.so_what: The customer identity of 'The Pragmatist' is defined on Amazon, but the brand has no platform to engage or deepen its relationship with this audience.

marker_17.so_what: The 'Safe Choice' social proof is strong on Amazon but is weakened by the lack of an official, stable brand presence on the web.
